guidelines.title: Diabetes Medication Dosing Guidelines
guidelines.content:
DIABETES MEDICATION DOSING GUIDELINES

METFORMIN (First-line for T2DM):
- Start: 500 mg daily or BID with meals
- Titrate: Increase by 500 mg weekly
- Target: 1000 mg BID (2000 mg/day)
- Maximum: 2550 mg/day (850 mg TID)
- Extended-release: 500-2000 mg once daily
- Renal: eGFR 30-45: max 1000 mg/day; eGFR <30: contraindicated
- Hold for contrast, surgery, acute illness

SGLT2 INHIBITORS:

Empagliflozin (Jardiance):
- T2DM: 10 mg daily, may increase to 25 mg
- Heart failure: 10 mg daily
- CKD: Can use to eGFR 20 (no glycemic effect <45)

Dapagliflozin (Farxiga):
- T2DM: 5 mg daily, may increase to 10 mg
- Heart failure/CKD: 10 mg daily
- Use to eGFR 25

Canagliflozin (Invokana):
- T2DM: 100 mg daily, may increase to 300 mg
- eGFR 30-60: 100 mg daily maximum
- eGFR <30: Avoid for glycemic control

GLP-1 RECEPTOR AGONISTS:

Semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy):
- Ozempic (T2DM): Start 0.25 mg SC weekly x4 weeks, then 0.5 mg, then 1 mg, max 2 mg weekly
- Wegovy (obesity): 0.25 mg weekly x4 weeks, escalate monthly to 2.4 mg weekly
- Oral (Rybelsus): 3 mg daily x30 days, then 7 mg, max 14 mg daily
  - Take on empty stomach with ≤4 oz water, wait 30 min before eating

Liraglutide (Victoza/Saxenda):
- Victoza: Start 0.6 mg SC daily x1 week, then 1.2 mg, max 1.8 mg
- Saxenda (obesity): 0.6 mg daily, escalate weekly by 0.6 mg to 3 mg daily

Dulaglutide (Trulicity):
- Start: 0.75 mg SC weekly
- May increase to 1.5 mg, then 3 mg, max 4.5 mg weekly

Tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound):
- Start: 2.5 mg SC weekly x4 weeks
- Escalate: 5 mg → 7.5 mg → 10 mg → 12.5 mg → 15 mg (monthly increments)
- Maximum: 15 mg weekly

DPP-4 INHIBITORS:

Sitagliptin (Januvia):
- 100 mg daily
- eGFR 30-45: 50 mg daily
- eGFR <30: 25 mg daily

Linagliptin (Tradjenta):
- 5 mg daily
- No renal adjustment needed

SULFONYLUREAS:

Glimepiride:
- Start: 1-2 mg daily with breakfast
- Maximum: 8 mg daily
- Elderly: Start 1 mg

Glipizide:
- Start: 2.5-5 mg daily
- Maximum: 20 mg daily (40 mg if divided)
- Give 30 min before meals

Glyburide:
- Start: 1.25-2.5 mg daily
- Maximum: 10 mg daily
- Higher hypoglycemia risk, avoid in elderly/CKD

THIAZOLIDINEDIONES:

Pioglitazone:
- Start: 15-30 mg daily
- Maximum: 45 mg daily
- Contraindicated in heart failure (NYHA III-IV)
- Monitor for edema, weight gain, fractures

INSULIN THERAPY:

Basal Insulin:
- NPH: Start 0.1-0.2 units/kg at bedtime or 10 units
- Glargine (Lantus/Basaglar): 10 units or 0.1-0.2 units/kg once daily
- Detemir (Levemir): Same, may need BID dosing
- Degludec (Tresiba): 10 units or 0.1-0.2 units/kg once daily

Titration:
- Increase by 2 units every 3 days (or 10-15% weekly)
- Target fasting glucose: 80-130 mg/dL

Prandial Insulin:
- Lispro (Humalog), Aspart (Novolog), Glulisine (Apidra):
  - Start: 4 units or 0.1 units/kg before largest meal
  - Or 10% of basal dose before each meal
  - Give 0-15 min before meals

- Regular insulin:
  - Give 30 min before meals
  - Onset slower, longer duration

Premixed Insulin:
- 70/30 (NPH/Regular): Start 10 units BID
- 75/25, 70/30 (analogs): Start 0.2-0.3 units/kg/day divided BID
- Give before breakfast and dinner

Insulin Adjustment Rules:
- Fasting high → Increase basal
- Pre-lunch high → Increase breakfast bolus
- Pre-dinner high → Increase lunch bolus
- Bedtime high → Increase dinner bolus
- 2 AM low → Decrease basal or bedtime bolus

DKA MANAGEMENT:

IV Fluids:
- 0.9% NS: 15-20 mL/kg/hour (1-1.5 L) first hour
- Then 250-500 mL/hour based on hydration
- Switch to 0.45% NS when Na normalizes
- Add D5 when glucose <200 mg/dL

Insulin:
- IV Regular: 0.1 units/kg bolus, then 0.1 units/kg/hour
- OR 0.14 units/kg/hour without bolus
- Target glucose drop: 50-75 mg/dL/hour
- When glucose <200: Reduce to 0.02-0.05 units/kg/hour

Potassium:
- K <3.3: Hold insulin, give 20-40 mEq/hour until >3.3
- K 3.3-5.3: 20-30 mEq per liter of fluids
- K >5.3: No potassium until <5.3

Bicarbonate:
- Only if pH <6.9: 100 mmol NaHCO3 in 400 mL water + 20 mEq KCl over 2 hours
- Repeat if pH still <7.0

EVIDENCE LEVEL: Level I (ADA Standards of Care 2024)

guidelines.title: Thyroid Medication Dosing Guidelines
guidelines.content:
THYROID MEDICATION DOSING GUIDELINES

HYPOTHYROIDISM:

Levothyroxine (Synthroid, Levoxyl):
- Full replacement: 1.6 mcg/kg/day (ideal body weight)
- Young healthy adults: Start full dose
- Elderly or cardiac disease: Start 12.5-25 mcg daily, increase by 12.5-25 mcg every 4-6 weeks
- Post-thyroidectomy/ablation: May need higher doses (2.0-2.2 mcg/kg)

Common doses by weight:
- 50 kg: ~80 mcg
- 70 kg: ~112 mcg
- 90 kg: ~144 mcg

Administration:
- Take on empty stomach, 30-60 min before breakfast
- Or 3-4 hours after dinner at bedtime
- Separate from calcium, iron, PPI by 4 hours
- Consistent timing important

TSH Targets:
- General: 0.5-2.5 mU/L
- Elderly: 4-6 mU/L may be acceptable
- Thyroid cancer (high-risk): 0.1-0.5 mU/L
- Thyroid cancer (low-risk): 0.5-2.0 mU/L
- Pregnancy: Trimester-specific

Adjustment Rules:
- TSH high: Increase by 12.5-25 mcg
- TSH low: Decrease by 12.5-25 mcg
- Recheck TSH in 6-8 weeks after any change
- Small changes (12.5 mcg) for TSH near goal

Liothyronine (T3 - Cytomel):
- Rarely needed for routine hypothyroidism
- 5-25 mcg daily in divided doses
- Some use T4/T3 combination (ratio 14-16:1)
- Myxedema coma: 5-20 mcg IV q8-12h

HYPERTHYROIDISM:

Methimazole (First-line):
- Graves' disease: Start 10-30 mg daily (depending on severity)
- Mild: 10-15 mg daily
- Moderate: 20-30 mg daily
- Severe: 30-40 mg daily (can divide)
- Maintenance: 5-10 mg daily
- Duration: 12-18 months, then reassess

Propylthiouracil (PTU):
- Reserved for first trimester pregnancy, thyroid storm, methimazole allergy
- 100-150 mg TID (300-450 mg/day)
- Thyroid storm: 200-400 mg q4h
- Higher hepatotoxicity risk than methimazole

Beta-Blockers for Symptoms:
- Propranolol: 10-40 mg TID-QID
- Atenolol: 25-100 mg daily
- Controls palpitations, tremor, anxiety
- Propranolol also inhibits T4→T3 conversion

Saturated Solution of Potassium Iodide (SSKI):
- Pre-surgery: 5-7 drops TID x7-10 days before thyroidectomy
- Thyroid storm: 5 drops q6h (give 1 hour after thionamide)
- Reduces thyroid vascularity and hormone release

THYROID STORM:

Treatment Protocol:
1. PTU 200-400 mg q4h (or methimazole 20 mg q4h)
2. SSKI 5 drops q6h (1 hour after PTU)
3. Propranolol 60-80 mg q4h (or esmolol drip)
4. Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV q8h
5. Cooling for fever (avoid aspirin - displaces T4)
6. Supportive care, ICU monitoring

MYXEDEMA COMA:

Treatment Protocol:
- T4 loading: 200-400 mcg IV
- T4 maintenance: 50-100 mcg IV daily
- Consider T3: 5-20 mcg IV q8-12h
- Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV q8h (until adrenal insufficiency ruled out)
- Supportive: Warming, fluids, ventilatory support
- Avoid aggressive rewarming (vasodilation)

THYROID NODULES/CANCER:

TSH Suppression (post-thyroidectomy):
- High-risk DTC: TSH <0.1 mU/L
- Intermediate-risk: TSH 0.1-0.5 mU/L
- Low-risk: TSH 0.5-2.0 mU/L
- Remission: TSH 0.5-2.0 mU/L (or lower normal)

Radioactive Iodine (I-131) Preparation:
- LID (low-iodine diet) x1-2 weeks
- Stop levothyroxine x4-6 weeks OR
- Thyrogen (rhTSH) 0.9 mg IM x2 days before RAI

EVIDENCE LEVEL: Level I (ATA Guidelines)

guidelines.title: Corticosteroid Dosing and Equivalence Guidelines
guidelines.content:
CORTICOSTEROID DOSING AND EQUIVALENCE GUIDELINES

GLUCOCORTICOID EQUIVALENCE:

Anti-inflammatory equivalence (approximate):
- Hydrocortisone 20 mg
- Cortisone 25 mg
- Prednisone 5 mg
- Prednisolone 5 mg
- Methylprednisolone 4 mg
- Triamcinolone 4 mg
- Dexamethasone 0.75 mg
- Betamethasone 0.6 mg

Mineralocorticoid Potency:
- Hydrocortisone: 1 (baseline)
- Prednisone: 0.8
- Methylprednisolone: 0.5
- Dexamethasone: 0
- Fludrocortisone: 200-250

Half-life Classification:
- Short-acting (8-12h): Hydrocortisone, cortisone
- Intermediate (12-36h): Prednisone, prednisolone, methylprednisolone
- Long-acting (36-72h): Dexamethasone, betamethasone

COMMON DOSING BY INDICATION:

Adrenal Insufficiency Replacement:

Hydrocortisone:
- Primary: 15-25 mg/day divided (2/3 AM, 1/3 afternoon)
- Typical: 10 mg AM, 5 mg afternoon, 5 mg evening
- OR 15 mg AM, 10 mg afternoon
- Stress dosing: Double or triple for illness

Prednisone (alternative):
- 3-5 mg daily (less physiologic)

Fludrocortisone (for primary AI):
- 0.05-0.2 mg daily
- Adjust for salt craving, orthostatic BP, K+

ACUTE ADRENAL CRISIS:
- Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus
- Then 50-100 mg IV q6-8h OR 200 mg/24h continuous
- IV fluids: 0.9% NS 1L first hour, then 250-500 mL/hour
- Identify and treat precipitant

STRESS DOSE STEROIDS:

Minor Stress (dental, minor outpatient):
- Hydrocortisone 25 mg or double daily dose
- Single dose day of procedure

Moderate Stress (cholecystectomy, orthopedic):
- Hydrocortisone 50-75 mg/day (or equivalent) x1-2 days
- OR 50 mg before, 25 mg q8h x24h

Major Stress (CABG, sepsis, major surgery):
- Hydrocortisone 100-150 mg/day x2-3 days
- Then rapid taper to baseline over 2-3 days
- OR 50 mg q6-8h day 1, then 25 mg q6h day 2, etc.

INFLAMMATORY CONDITIONS:

Rheumatoid Arthritis:
- Low-dose prednisone: 5-10 mg daily as bridge
- Taper as DMARDs take effect

SLE Flare:
- Mild: Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day
- Moderate: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day
- Severe (nephritis): Methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg IV x3 days, then PO

Asthma Exacerbation:
- Prednisone 40-60 mg daily x5-7 days
- No taper needed if <10 days

COPD Exacerbation:
- Prednisone 40 mg daily x5 days
- Shorter courses preferred

Gout Flare:
- Prednisone 30-35 mg daily x5 days
- OR tapering: 30 mg x3 days, 20 mg x3 days, 10 mg x3 days

ITP:
- Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (max 80 mg)
- Taper when platelets >50,000

Allergic Reactions:
- Prednisone 40-60 mg daily x3-5 days
- Taper if used >7-10 days

Multiple Sclerosis Relapse:
- Methylprednisolone 1000 mg IV daily x3-5 days
- OR high-dose oral equivalent

TAPERING GUIDELINES:

When to Taper:
- Used >2-3 weeks
- High doses (≥20 mg prednisone)
- Signs of HPA suppression
- AM cortisol <5 mcg/dL

General Taper Approach:
- >40 mg prednisone: Reduce by 10 mg every 1-2 weeks
- 20-40 mg: Reduce by 5 mg every 1-2 weeks
- 10-20 mg: Reduce by 2.5 mg every 2-4 weeks
- <10 mg: Reduce by 1 mg every 2-4 weeks
- Slower taper for longer duration of use

ADVERSE EFFECTS MONITORING:
- Glucose (weekly initially if diabetic/high-risk)
- Blood pressure
- Weight, appetite
- Mood, sleep
- Bone density (if long-term use anticipated)
- Ophthalmology for cataracts/glaucoma
- Calcium/vitamin D supplementation if >3 months use

EVIDENCE LEVEL: Level I (Endocrine Society guidelines)
